family_audit: Close the forced-3-CTA 128x128 occupancy family across both control paths: the PTX probe and the non-PTX sibling probe both reached about 168 registers and about 24.7% active warps, but both also drove barrier stall to about 11% and regressed into the 25.9-26.4 ms range.
Treat the launch-bounds occupancy idea on 128x128 as sufficiently answered for now: higher residency alone is not enough when it arrives by inflating synchronization cost.
Recommend an exact PTX restore again because the live surface is sitting at 26.40 ms and the loop should not stack another aggressive edit on top of a known-bad base.

Promote PTX-local barrier surgery as the most evidence-backed next aggressive family after recovery, because it now explains both negative 128x128 launch-bounds branches better than simple occupancy does.
Keep the 256x128 low-register transplant in the live queue as the broader high-ceiling family.

directions.name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
directions.hypothesis: Round 29 through round 32 are now enough evidence to step off the forced-3-CTA 128x128 family and recover. The PTX launch-bounds probe and the non-PTX sibling launch-bounds probe both converged on the same answer: they lowered the hot-band register budget to 168, raised achieved warps to about 24.7%, and collapsed long-scoreboard stalls, but they also pushed barrier stall to roughly 11% and regressed runtime into the mid-20s. That means the loop should return to the exact 489574e PTX anchor now so the next aggressive branch starts from the best correctness-proven base instead of from a measured loss.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Known register-limited plateau on the accepted PTX hot-band surface, used here as a recovery anchor.
directions.risk: Low. This is the clean recovery path.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the current 26.398208 ms run
ranking_notes: The current live surface is again a measured regression, so recovery is the right first move.
Use the restored result as a clean base for the next aggressive branch; do not interpret it as new progress.
directions.stop_condition: Treat a restored 24.16-24.18 ms result as recovery only and attach a fresh structural change in the following round.

directions.name: Trim PTX Microkernel Barriers Without Reintroducing Shared-Memory Blowup
directions.hypothesis: The last four rounds isolate the next 128x128 PTX-local target very cleanly. Forcing residency on both the PTX and non-PTX 128x128 surfaces lowered registers and raised active warps, but both branches died on the same synchronization wall. The x32 follow-on proved that the answer is not to double the shared-memory footprint. That leaves one most-evidence-backed aggressive family after recovery: keep the exact PTX anchor and its 22,016 B shared-memory footprint, but surgically reduce the wait/sync cadence or export handoff so barrier cost falls without giving back the small-shared-memory advantage.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Barrier cadence inside the single-K PTX microkernel after the residency experiments exposed synchronization as the real limiting cost.
directions.risk: High. This is microkernel-level control-flow work and is easier to get wrong than a restore or a launch-bounds-only probe.
ranking_notes: This is now the most directly evidenced aggressive family in the repo because both 128x128 launch-bounds negatives point at the same barrier wall.
It stays below the restore because round 33 should not stack another aggressive edit on a regressed live surface.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the edit reintroduces x32-like shared-memory inflation or fails to reduce barrier stall materially from the ~11% seen on the launch-bounds probes.

directions.name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
directions.hypothesis: The 256x128 family still matters because it is the only route in repo history that ever pushed active warps into the low-30% range. But it remains behind the PTX-local barrier-surgery family because its implementation surface is broader and its correctness history is worse.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register footprint and synchronization strategy on the 256x128 path, plus correctness-sensitive writer ownership.
directions.risk: High. This remains the broadest and most correctness-sensitive active family.
ranking_notes: Keep this family live because it still carries the highest visible occupancy ceiling in repo history.
Rank it below PTX-local barrier surgery because the latter now has more direct causal evidence and a smaller implementation surface.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately on correctness failure or on another 28-30 ms class regression without a decisive low-30%-warps breakthrough.

notes: Round 33 groups the PTX and non-PTX 128x128 launch-bounds probes into one coherent negative family. Both branches reached about 168 registers, about 24.7% active warps, and about 3 CTA residency, and both regressed because barrier stall climbed to about 11%. The x32 follow-on also already showed that doubling shared memory to amortize those barriers is not acceptable. The right move now is to restore the exact PTX anchor and then spend the next aggressive budget on true barrier surgery or on the broader 256x128 low-register branch, not on another launch-bounds replay.
